coredata.dc:title: In situ-generated H2O2 with NCQDs/MIL-101(Fe) by activating O2: A dual effect of photocatalysis and photo-Fenton for efficient removal of tetracyline at natural pH
coredata.dc:creator: Xie, Yuanhong
Liu, Chenrui
Li, Dejian
Liu, Yun
coredata.dc:description:
Herein, a novel composite of MIL-101(Fe) loaded with nitrogen doped carbon quantum dots (NCQDs/MIL-101(Fe), denoted as NQML), which can construct dual effect of photocatalysis and photo-Fenton for organic pollutants removal, was prepared by solvent impregnation method. Characterization methods proved that NQML had been synthesized successfully. Additionally, the adding volume of NCQDs could effectively influence the photocatalytic activities of NQML-x composites (x represents the adding volume of NCQDs) for tetracycline (TC) degradation, and NQML-8 exhibited the optimum photocatalytic performance among the studied catalysts upon visible light illumination under natural pH, which can completely degrade TC within 180 min. By comparison, the TC degradation kinetic constant in the system with NQML-8 was as 15.85 and 8.24 folds of that in the NCQDs and MIL-101(Fe) systems, respectively. The mechanism study proved that the enhanced photocatalytic performance of NQML was resulted from the visible light up-conversion property, electron-hole separation and transfer capacity of NCQDs. Meanwhile, NQML could not only produce h+ but also lead to in-situ generation of O2
- and H2O2 by activation of O2 under visible light. The generated H2O2 could react with Fe(II) in MIL-101(Fe) to produce
OH so as to construct a dual effect of photocatalysis and photo-Fenton in TC removal.

head.ce:title: In situ-generated H2O2 with NCQDs/MIL-101(Fe) by activating O2: A dual effect of photocatalysis and photo-Fenton for efficient removal of tetracyline at natural pH

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Environmental Science and Engineering, College of Environment and Resources, Xiangtan University, Xiangtan 411105, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Environmental Science and Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Environmental Science and Engineering, College of Environment and Resources, Xiangtan University, Xiangtan 411105, China

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: Yuanhong Xie and Chenrui Liu contributed equally to this work.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para:
Herein, a novel composite of MIL-101(Fe) loaded with nitrogen doped carbon quantum dots (NCQDs/MIL-101(Fe), denoted as NQML), which can construct dual effect of photocatalysis and photo-Fenton for organic pollutants removal, was prepared by solvent impregnation method. Characterization methods proved that NQML had been synthesized successfully. Additionally, the adding volume of NCQDs could effectively influence the photocatalytic activities of NQML-x composites (x represents the adding volume of NCQDs) for tetracycline (TC) degradation, and NQML-8 exhibited the optimum photocatalytic performance among the studied catalysts upon visible light illumination under natural pH, which can completely degrade TC within 180 min. By comparison, the TC degradation kinetic constant in the system with NQML-8 was as 15.85 and 8.24 folds of that in the NCQDs and MIL-101(Fe) systems, respectively. The mechanism study proved that the enhanced photocatalytic performance of NQML was resulted from the visible light up-conversion property, electron-hole separation and transfer capacity of NCQDs. Meanwhile, NQML could not only produce h+ but also lead to in-situ generation of O2
- and H2O2 by activation of O2 under visible light. The generated H2O2 could react with Fe(II) in MIL-101(Fe) to produce
OH so as to construct a dual effect of photocatalysis and photo-Fenton in TC removal.

ce:section.ce:para: Since Alexander Fleming found penicillin inthe1920s [1], hundreds of antibiotics have appeared on the market and played important roles in taking precautions againstand treating human and animal diseases. As a kind of cheap broad-spectrum antibiotic, tetracycline (TC) is extensively applied to various aspects such as food industry, agriculture and aquaculture. With the wide applications of TC, it has been commonly identified in rivers, soil and even groundwater, resulting in potential risks such as kidney disease, kidney mutations and teratogenic effects to human bodies [2]. Accordingly, a proper approach should be urgently needed to remove TC from natural water and wastewater.

Up to now, several methods have been studied to remove pollutants from water, such as adsorption [3], biological methods [4], chemical oxidations [5,6], etc. Adsorption methods are simple and efficient but they cannot effectively degrade the target pollutants. Biological methods have the advantages of low treatment cost, and easy cultivation of microorganisms, but they are inefficient for TC degradation due to their low biodegradability. Compared with them, chemical oxidations not only can overcome the above problems, but also possess the traits of superior degradation ability towards organic pollutants and environmental friendliness. Under this circumstances, some requirements play significant roles in chemical oxidation: (1) the oxidant is environmentally friendly; (2) an excellent degradation rate can be maintained under neutral conditions [7]. It is well-known that molecular oxygen (O2) could be an ideal oxidizing agent for wastewater treatment. Therefore, many chemical oxidation methods such as photocatalysis [8], microwave methods [9], electrochemistry [10], and microbial fuel cells [11] have been reported to activate O2 to generate reactive oxygen species (ROSs), which have been adopted to the disposal of organic contaminants. The unique benefit can make these methods promising and potential in the field of water purification. Among the above methods, the activation of O2 via photocatalytic method has gained widespread attention due to its accessible operating process and mild reactive conditions. For example, Geng et al.
[12] demonstrated that ZnO/g-C3N4 heterojunction photocatalyst could activate O2 to in situ produce O2
- and H2O2 under simulated sunlight. Then H2O2 was photolyzed into
OH, whose redox potential was 2.8 eV [13]. Therefore, O2
-, H2O2 and
OH were proved to be the dominated ROSs in this photocatalytic sterilization system (Eqs.
(1)–(3) ).
(1) O2 + e-→ O2
-
(2) O2 + 2H+ + 2e-→ H2O2
(3) H2O2 + hv →
OH

However, H2O2 is not considered to be an ideal oxidant compared with
OH, because it has a relatively lower redox potential than
OH and usually needs to be photolyzed into
OH at the wavelength below 300 nm [14]. Thus, it is vital to explore an appropriate way to covert the in situ-generated H2O2 into
OH effectively. Recently, some approaches had been carried out to activate H2O2 produced in photocatalytic systems by adding external Fe2+ source to construct a photo-Fenton reaction. For instance, Jiang et al.
[15] reported that the photocatalytic and photo-Fenton removal of phenol by CdS/rGO/Fe2+ under circumneutral pH. The in situ-produced H2O2 by CdS/rGO could be utilized by external Fe2+ source (FeSO4·7H2O), further accelerating the photocatalytic reaction. Nevertheless, the external Fe2+ addition may result in the loss of iron ions, which was not conducive to the recycling of the catalyst. In this case, we wonder whether some iron-containing photocatalyst could be taken into consideration to construct both heterogeneous photocatalytic and photo-Fenton systems, in which the in situ-generated H2O2 can be captured by the photocatalyst itself.

Lately, metal organic framework (MOFs) materials have been widely used in many fields such as adsorption [16,17], catalytic oxidation [18–20] and photocatalysis [21,22] owing to their controllable pore size, modified pore surface, high surface area and superior light response. As a typical MOFs material, Materials Institute Lavoisier-101(Fe) (MIL-101(Fe)) is assembled by Fe3+ and organic ligands, whose internal Fe-O clusters make it possess a good absorption under ultraviolet and short-wavelength visible light (200 nm ∼ 600 nm) [23], thus becoming a promising catalyst in the field of photocatalysis. For example, Zhang et al.
[24] proposed that carbon fiber/TiO2/MIL-101(Fe) composite could be applied to remove 17β-estradiol and TC under visible light, in which the loading of MIL-101(Fe) could improve the absorption ability of carbon fiber/TiO2 composite in the wavelength of 200 nm ∼ 600 nm. Nonetheless, MIL-101(Fe) displays poor electron/hole (e-/h+) separation ability and limited absorption ability at the long-wavelength in visible region (600 nm ∼ 800 nm), which makes its visible light photocatalytic efficiency still not satisfactory [25]. Thus, it is vital to boost the separation efficiency of e-/h+ and excavate the utilization potential of MIL-101(Fe) towards the long wavelength of visible region (600 nm ∼ 800 nm). Recently, carbon quantum dots (CQDs) have aroused wide discussions by virtue of low cost, non-toxicity and simple operation. As a kind of typical carbon nanomaterial, CQDs are composed of discrete quasi-spherical nanoparticles with the size of below 10 nm. Furthermore, their superior electrons storage abilities could be conducive to establish a center of photogenerated carriers [26]. More importantly, CQDs can convert long-wavelength light in visible region into short-wavelength even ultraviolet light owing to their up-conversion properties [27], so as to promote the utilization rate of semiconductors towards long-wavelength light. Nevertheless, the application of CQDs may be hindered due to suffering from the poor transfer ability of carriers and limited photoluminescence performance. Lately, nitrogen-doped CQDs (NCQDs) have been proposed to effectively improve the carriers transfer ability and photoluminescence performance of CQDs [28]. In addition, they can still retain the up-conversion properties of CQDs, thus accelerating their photocatalytic activities [29].

Inspired by the above discussion, in this study, MIL-101(Fe) loaded with NCQDs (NCQDs/MIL-101(Fe), denoted as NQML) were synthesized by solvent impregnation method. Systematic characterization methods were applied to evidence the structure and properties of the as-synthesized catalyst. The photocatalytic performance and mechanism of NQML were also studied in detail. A dual effect of photocatalysis and photo-Fenton of NQML were expected to establish in this system. Finally, the possible chemical structure of the intermediate products was discussed in the degradation process of TC. This study may provide a theoretical basis for constructing a dual effect of photocatalytic and photo-Fenton processes toward the treatment of organic contaminants.

The TC (C22H24N2O8·xH2O, 98%), terephthalic acid (H2BDC, C8H6O4), HNO3, NaOH, P-benzoquinone (BQ, C6H4O2), NH4F, Phenanthroline (C12H10N2O), 2,9-Dimethyl-1,10-phenanthroline (C14H15ClN2O), Na2CO3, urea (H2NCONH2) were purchased from Macklin Reagent Company. FeCl3·6H2O, N, N-dimethylformamide (DMF, 99.5%), dichloromethane (CH2Cl2), acetic acid (C2H4O2), isopropanol (C3H8O), 5,5-Dimethyl-1-pyrrolineN-oxide (C6H11NO), CH3COONH4, CuSO4·5H2O, NaH2PO4, KH2PO4, K2HPO4·3H2O and Na2SO4 were supplied by Aladdin Industrial Corporation (Shanghai, China). (NH4)2C2O4, CH3CH2OH and citric acid (C6H8O7·H2O) were provided by Hunan Huihong Reagent Co., Ltd. Ultrapure water was utilized in the whole experiments.

MIL-101(Fe) was synthesized by a modified solvothermal method as described previously [30]. Briefly, 1.35 g FeCl3·6H2O and 0.42 g H2BDC were dissolved in 60 mL DMF, followed by sonication for 30 min to confirm that the solids were completely dissolved. The obtained orange transparent solution was sealed in a 100 mL Teflon-lined stainless steel autoclave and heated at 110 °C for 20 h. Then, the obtained dark orange solid product was collected by centrifugation (7000 r/min, 5 min) and washed for several times using DMF and hot ethanol. Afterwards, the purified products were dried overnight at 70 °C.

NCQDs were obtained using a hydrothermal method based on the previous work with subtle modifications [31]. 3.00 g citric acid and 1.45 g carbamide were dispersed into a 15 mL ultrapure water to obtain a colorless mixture. Afterwards, the above mixture was added into a 100 mL stainless steel autoclave and heated at 180 °C for 5 h. After cooling down, the centrifugation of solution to remove any large particles was followed. Whereafter, the solution was dialyzed using a dialysis membrane (MWCO = 1 KDa) in ultrapure water for 3 days to obtain a light-yellow NCQDs solution.

NQML hybrid photocatalyst was prepared by loading NCQDs onto MIL-101(Fe) via solvent impregnation method (Fig. 1 ) according to the previous work [32]. 1.00 g MIL-101(Fe) powder and designated volume of NCQDs solution (2, 4, 6, 8, 10 and 12 mL, respectively) were mixed with 100 mL deionized water. The above-mentioned mixtures were sonicated for 30 min, stirred in a water bath at 90 °C for 3 h, then centrifuged. Finally, the materials obtained from those mixtures were placed in an oven and dried overnight at 70 °C, which were labeled as NQML-2, NQML-4, NQML-6, NQML-8, NQML-10, NQML-12, respectively.
The detailed processes of the characterization tests and photocatalytic degradation experiments of catalysts were described in the Text S1 of Supporting Information (SI).

As seen from Fig. 2
a (inset), the XRD spectrum of NCQDs demonstrates a broad characteristic peak between the diffraction angles of 15° and 30°, which is related to the graphitic carbon [33]. Fig. 2 a illustrates the XRD patterns of MIL-101(Fe) and NQML-x composites (x represents the volume of NCQDs). The distinct diffraction peaks appearing at 9.31°, 12.32°, 16.18°, 18.94° and 21.96° are all attributed to MIL-101(Fe), which is in accord with previous literature [34]. Furthermore, the characteristic signals of MIL-101(Fe) can all be found in the patterns of NQML-x. However, a faint broad peak of NCQDs can also be observed around 25° in these composites, suggesting that NCQDs were successfully loaded onto MIL-101(Fe) without changing its crystal phase significantly. The FT-IR spectra of NCQDs, MIL-101(Fe) and NQML-x composites are displayed in Fig. 2 b. The peak at 3435 cm−1 is observed in all samples, belonging to the bending vibration peaks of the O-H bond of water molecules on the sample surface [35]. As for NCQDs, three characteristic peaks located at 1710, 1631 and 1383 cm−1 are belonging to the CO bond stretching vibration peak, the CC plane bending vibration peak and CN stretching vibration peak, respectively [36], which may be resulted from the large number of carboxyl groups on NCQDs surface. With regard to MIL-101(Fe), the peaks at 1580 and 1405 cm−1 are assigned to the symmetrical and anti-symmetric stretching vibration peaks of the CO bond in the carboxyl group, while the peak at 1692 and 746 cm−1 are corresponding to the stretching vibration peaks of the CC bond and CH bond on the benzene ring of MIL-101(Fe). Moreover, the absorption peak at 549 cm−1 is corresponding to the stretching vibration of FeO bond formed by the coordination of trivalent iron ions and carboxyl groups in MIL-101(Fe) [35]. For NQML-x composites, the peaks belonging to NCQDs and MIL-101(Fe) can be detected simultaneously, which further proves that NCQDs has been successfully loaded onto MIL-101(Fe).

XPS measurements were applied to further characterize the surface chemical compositions of NCQDs, MIL-101(Fe) and NQML-x, in which NQML-8 was chosen as the typical catalyst due to its highest photocatalytic performance (asindicatedbelow). The full survey spectra ( Fig. S1 ) reveal the presence of C, N and O elements in NCQDs, as well as the existence of Fe, O, C and N elements in MIL-101(Fe) and NQML-8 materials simultaneously. The Fe2p spectrum of MIL-101(Fe) (Fig. 2 c) presents two peaks at 711.3 and 713.7 eV, belonging to the characteristic peaks of Fe(III)O bond [37]. However, in the pattern of NQML-8, the corresponding peaks have been shifted to 711.2 and 712.7 eV, respectively, which may be caused by the generation of Fe-O-C bond between NCQDs and MIL-101(Fe) [38]. The high-resolution O1s XPS spectrum of NCQDs (Fig. 2 d) presents two peaks at 533.4 and 531.7 eV, which are related to the COH and CO bonds, respectively [39]. Additionally, the FeO peak also appears at 529.9 eV in the spectra of MIL-101(Fe) and NQML-8 [40]. In the C1s spectra of the three samples (Fig. 2 e), it can be observed that three deconvoluted peaks at 284.5, 284.9 and 288.7 eV are corresponding to the CO, CC and CN bonds of NCQDs, respectively [41]. Moreover, The C-N peak of NQML-8 shifts to the lower binding energy compared with that of pure NQML-8, indicating that its electron density is increased, which could promote its photocatalytic activity [42]. The above evidence further supports the FT-IR results and confirms that the NCQDs was successfully combined with MIL-101(Fe). Furthermore, the peaks at 400.0 and 401.2 eV are belonging to C-N and N-H bonds in NCQDs, respectively (Fig. 2 f). Compared with NCQDs, the C-N peaks in the MIL-101(Fe) and NQML-8 have shifted to the higher binding energy, suggesting a strong interaction occurred between NCQDs and MIL-101(Fe), which is benefit for the charge transfer between them [43].

The surface morphology of NCQDs, MIL-101(Fe) and NQML-8 can be studied by SEM and TEM measurements. The SEM and TEM images (Fig. 3
a-b) show that NCQDs have spherical shapes with narrow particle size distributions ranging from 1 to 11 nm. Besides, the lattice spacing of NCQDs is about 0.23 nm (Fig. 3 c), which is in accordance with the (100) crystal plane of graphite, representing that NCQDs possesses a crystalline graphitic carbon structure [44]. As shown in Fig. 3 d-f, MIL-101(Fe) shows a regular octahedral morphology with smooth surface and uniform size varying from 0.30 to 1.50 µm, moreover, the spacing of lattice fringes for MIL-101(Fe) is 0.34 nm. After loaded with NCQDs, MIL-101(Fe) maintains its regular octahedral shape and the same value of lattice spacing, but its surface becomes relatively rough (Fig. 3 g-i). This result further indicates the loading of NCQDs cannot change the crystal properties of MIL-101(Fe). Moreover, the elemental distribution and composition of NQML-8 were further confirmed by EDS mapping. As shown in Fig. 3 j-o, four main elements of Fe, C, O, N with homogeneous distribution are detected in the composite. This result manifests NCQDs are uniformly loaded on the surface of NQML-8.

Photoluminescence spectra and UV–vis DRS were employed to explore the optical absorption characteristics of NCQDs, MIL-101(Fe) and NQML-x. As seen from Fig. 4
a, the NCQDs display two characteristic absorption peaks located at 240 and 342 nm, respectively, which is caused by the absorptions of aromatic π systems [48]. Moreover, NCQDs exhibits a bright blue luminescence when excited under visible light, which can promote the migration rates of electron/hole pairs during photocatalytic process (Fig. 4 a (inset)) [49]. The up-conversion fluorescent property can be further confirmed by Fig. 4 b, the emission peaks of NCQDs have been found in the range of 200 and 600 nm at the excitation wavelength of 700 ∼ 800 nm, and the best excitation wavelength is 780 nm (Fig. 4 b), reflecting the up-conversion photoluminescence property of NCQDs. Meanwhile, UV–vis DRS of MIL-101(Fe) and NQML-x are presented in Fig. 4 c, which illustrates that MIL-101(Fe) and NQML-x can absorb both ultraviolet light and visible light. Moreover, NQML-x display obviously higher light absorption ability at the wavelength of 200 nm to 600 nm than that of MIL-101(Fe), which indicates the higher absorption ability of MIL-101(Fe) after loading NCQDs. Although limited increment of adsorption in NQML compared with MIL-101(Fe) in the wavelength range of 600 nm to 800 nm, NCQDs can convert the near-infrared light to short-wavelength visible light or even ultraviolet light owing to the up-conversion photoluminescence properties, thus promoting the light adsorption ability of MIL-101(Fe).

The photocatalytic performances of the synthesized catalysts to degrade TC under visible light was evaluated. As Fig. 5
A displays, limited TC degradation (17.23%) is observed in the situation with only visible light (curve a), suggesting that TC is extremely stable upon visible light illumination at natural pH conditions. Under dark conditions (curve b and d), the TC removal rates in NCQDs and MIL-101(Fe) systems are only 17.52% and 40.13% within 270 min, respectively. These results demonstrate that MIL-101(Fe) has strong absorption ability towards TC, owing to its huge specific surface area. While under visible light, the TC degradation efficiencies in NCQDs (curve c) and MIL-101(Fe) (curve e) systems raise up to 27.12% and 76.25%, respectively, which verifies that NCQDs and MIL-101(Fe) exhibit good response to visible light. For comparison, the NQML-x composites (curves g-l) show significantly enhanced TC degradation rate than MIL-101(Fe), illustrating that NCQDs plays a significant part in accelerating the photoactivity of these composites. Moreover, it should be noted that the photocatalytic performance of the NQML-x composites gradually increases when the volume of NCQDs increases from 2 to 8, in which NQML-8 can completely degrade TC within 180 min. However, the photocatalytic performance decreases somewhat with further augmenting the content of NCQDs, which may be related to the reason that the relative content of MIL-101(Fe) is reduced when NCQDs is overloaded. For the sake of deeply assessing the superiority of MIL-101(Fe) on the degradation effect of TC after loaded with NCQDs, the kinetic simulations on the TC degradation in different systems were conducted (Fig. 5 B). The TC degradation kinetic constants in the “NCQDs + vis” and “MIL-101(Fe) + vis” systems are 1.30 × 10-3 and 2.50 × 10-3 min−1, respectively. While in “NQML-8 + vis” system, it can be up to 20.6 × 10-3 min−1, which is 15.85 and 8.24 times of that in “NCQDs + vis” and “MIL-101(Fe) + vis” systems, respectively. It can be concluded from the above results that NQML-8 possesses the most excellent photocatalytic performance among these studied materials. Therefore, the follow-up experiments were carried out with NQML-8 as the representative photocatalyst.

Considering the ubiquitous variety of inorganic anions and organics in natural water, the effects of different representative inorganic anions (SO4 2-, Cl-, HPO4 2-, HCO3 –) and organics (human acid (HA)) on the degradation efficiency of TC by NQML-8 were discussed. The concentration of these inorganic anions and organics were determined by the indexes of natural water according to the previous studies [50,51]. As illustrated in Fig. 5 C, the catalytic abilities of NQML-8 towards TC have slightly decreased in the existence of inorganic anions (SO4 2-, Cl-, HPO4 2-, HCO3 –), whose removal efficiencies are 88.6%, 86.4%, 90.5% and 91.8%, respectively. This result may be ascribed to the fact that some of ROSs and h+ are transformed to other less active species by these inorganic ions according to the following reactions [52,53] (Eqs.
(4)–(10) ). Furthermore, the TC degradation has also decreased to 80.2% in the presence of HA, which may be resulted from the competition of radicals between HA and TC molecules [23]. Based on the above results, the removal efficiencies of TC by NQML-8 are only decreased slightly in the coexistence of different inorganic anions and organics, signifying a good practical application of NQML-8 in TC treatment.
(4) SO4 2- + h+ → SO4
-
(5) SO4 2- +
OH → SO4
- + OH–
(6) Cl- + h+ → Cl2
-
(7) Cl- +
OH → ClOH -
(8) HPO4 2- +
OH → HPO4
- + OH–
(9) HCO3 – +
OH → CO3
- + H2O
(10) HCO3 – + h+ → HCO3

The photocatalytic stability of NQML-8 was further evaluated by cycling experiments, and the optimal experimental conditions were determined by the influencingfactors experiments (as shown in Text S2 and Figs. S3-S6 of SI). As depicted in Fig. 5 D, NQML-8 still exhibits excellent photocatalytic performance after four cycles, whose TC photocatalytic removal rate remains about 92.21% after 240 min reaction. In addition, the leaching concentration of iron ions during the reaction was quantitatively analyzed (Fig. 5 E). In MIL-101(Fe) and NQML-8 systems, the maximum leaching Fe concentrations are only 0.83 and 0.69 ppm, respectively. More importantly, the leaching iron ion concentration in “NQML-8 + vis” system is always lower than that of “MIL-101(Fe) + vis” system in entire reaction process, suggesting that the loading of NCQDs promotes the structural stability of MIL-101(Fe).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Reactive oxidizing species and reaction mechanism
ce:section.ce:para:
The band gap energies (Eg) of MIL-101(Fe) and NQML-8 were conducted by the Tauc plots, which were deduced from the UV–vis DRS of the two photocatalysts according to the following equation: (αhv)1/2 = A(hv-Eg), where α, h, v, A and Eg refer to absorption coefficient, Planck’s constant, frequency, constant and the band gap of the semiconductor, respectively [54]. From the Tauc plots (Fig. 6
a), the band gaps of MIL-101(Fe) and NQML-8 are estimated to be 2.72 and 2.61 eV, respectively. It says that the narrower band gap can boost the generation of e-/h+ pairs more easily under visible light [55]. Thus, it can be easier to generate e-/h+ pairs in NQML-8 than in MIL-101(Fe) upon visible light exposure. Additionally, the energy band structures of MIL-101(Fe) and NQML-8 were measured by Mott-Schottky plots and VB-XPS spectra, so as to further study the mechanism about photoinduced carrier separation. Based on the Mott-Schottky plots (Fig. 6 b), the flat-band potentials (Efb) of MIL-101(Fe) and NQML-8 are determined to be −0.88 and −0.69 V (vs. Ag/AgCl), which are equivalent to −0.68 and −0.49 V (vs. NHE), respectively [56]. And the Fermi levels (Ef) of MIL-101(Fe) and NQML-8 are deduced to be −0.68 and −0.49 eV (vs. NHE), respectively [57]. The VB-XPS spectra (Fig. 6 c) reveal that the Evf (thebindingenergyoftheonsetedge) for the MIL-101(Fe) and NQML-8 can be calculated to be 1.88 and 1.63 eV, respectively. Therefore, the VB potentials of MIL-101(Fe) and NQML-8 are determined to be 1.20 and 1.14 V in the light of the equation: EVB = Ef + Evf [58]. Thus, the CB positions of the MIL-101(Fe) and NQML-8 are −1.52 and −1.47 V (ECB = EVB-Eg), respectively. According to the aforementioned information, the energy band alignments of the MIL-101(Fe) and NQML-8 are illustrated schematically, which are shown in Fig. 6 d. Interestingly, the CB, VB and Ef positions of MIL-101(Fe) have all been changed with the introduction of NCQDs. Particularly, when NCQDs is in close contact with MIL-101 (Fe), the electrons generated from MIL-101 (Fe) can migrate to NCQDs due to the existence of the Schottky barrier [59], which can shorten the migration path of photogenerated electrons. This phenomenon can raise the photocatalytic capability of the composite.

PL spectroscopy, I-T curves and electrochemical impedance spectra (EIS) Nyquist plots were applied to further assess the separation ability and transfer rate of photocarriers. PL spectroscopy was employed to analyze the separation efficiency of e-/h+ pairs, which is generally considered that the stronger fluorescence intensity represents the more recombination of e-/h+ pairs and lower photocatalytic performance [60]. As presented in Fig. 7
a, MIL-101(Fe) and NQML-8 both have an emission peak around 450 nm when the excitation wavelength is 360 nm. However, the PL intensity of NQML-8 is much lower compared with MIL-101(Fe), demonstrating that the more effective e-/h+ separation of NQML-8. The I-T curves were used to further identify the electron-hole separation efficiency (Fig. 7 b). As expected, a higher photocurrent response can be obtained for MIL-101(Fe) by comparison with MIL-101(Fe), which further demonstrates that NQML-8 displays more excellent electron-hole separation rate than that of MIL-101(Fe) [61]. The EIS Nyquist plots were applied to evaluate the transfer rate of photocarriers. It can be seen from Fig. 7 c that the arc radius of the EIS Nyquist plot of NQML-8 is less than that of MIL-101(Fe), signifying that the NQML-8 owns a lower electron transfer resistance [62]. In order to quantitatively compare the electron transfer resistances of MIL-101(Fe) and NQML-8, an equivalent circuit Rs(Q1R1)(Q2R2) (Fig. 7 d) were employed to fit these EIS plots using ZSimpWin 3.00 software. In this circuit, Rs is the resistance of solution, Q1 and Q2 represent pseudo-capacitance, R1 and R2 stand for the charge transfer resistance and diffusion resistance [63]. As summarized in Table 2 , the charge transfer resistance of NQML-8 (1.1 × 105/Ω cm2) is less than that of MIL-101(Fe) (1.5 × 105/Ω cm2), further proving the faster electron transfer rate of NQML-8 compared with MIL-101(Fe).

As aforementioned, photocatalytic reaction plays an important role in activating O2 that can produce ROSs. Hence, the trapping experiments of ROSs and h+ generated by NQML-8 under visible light were conducted to appraise the contributions of different active species in the photocatalytic degradation of TC. Therein, IPA, AO and BQ were carried out as the captureagents of
OH, h+ and O2
-, respectively [64]. As Fig. S8
a shows, after adding 1, 5 and 10 mM IPA during the reaction, the degradation effect of NCQDs/MIL-101(Fe)-8 on TC is inhibited to varying degrees. After adding an excessive amount of IPA (10 mM), the inhibition rate of the reaction system is 32.25%. Fig. S8
b appears that the degradation efficiency of TC has the highest inhibited rate after adding 10 mM AO, whose inhibition rate can reach up to 25.62%. As illustrated in Fig. S8
c, the TC degradation is inhibited about 46.31% when BQ is excessive (10 mM). Thus, it can be concluded from the above analysis that
OH, h+ and O2
- all make primary contributions to the removal of TC.

In order to further verify the ability of NQML-8 to generate free radicals for TC degradation, the
OH and O2
- in this system were identified by using the electron spin resonance (ESR) with DMPO. For comparison, the corresponding ROSs in MIL-101(Fe) system were also detected. As displayed in Figs. 7e-f, a signal with an intensity ratio of 1:2:2:1 for the DMPO-
OH and a signal with characteristic intensity ratio of 1:1:1:1 for DMPO-O2
- can be found in these systems [65], suggesting that
OH and O2
- are produced in both the MIL-101(Fe) and NQML-8 photocatalytic systems. Moreover, the signals of DMPO-
OH and DMPO-O2
- in the NQML-8 system are significantly stronger than those in the MIL-101(Fe) system, which reveals that more
OH and O2
- could be generated following the loading of NCQDs.

According to the previous discussion, it can be obtained that dissolved oxygen occupies a significant position in the formation of
OH and O2
-. Hence, to explore the TC degradation efficiency in the system with NQML-8 under anoxic conditions, the photocatalytic performance of the prepared catalyst under nitrogen atmosphere was carried out. As illustrated in Fig. S7
d, the TC degradation efficiency is 100% in the natural environment (dissolved oxygen concentration is 4.37 mg/L). However, the corresponding removal rate of TC reduces to 64.21% in nitrogen atmosphere. Particularly, after achieving the adsorption equilibrium, the TC degradation rate reaches to 26.05% in the photocatalytic period under nitrogen atmosphere, which is close to the result of the trapping experiment of h+. The obtained results can be explained that h+ plays the dominate role in removing TC under nitrogen atmosphere, further indicating that the
OH and O2
- originate from O2 in this photocatalytic system.

Nevertheless, it should be emphasized that the valence band potential of the catalyst NQML-8 is 1.14 eV, which is lower than the oxidation potential of
OH/H2O (+1.99 eV). In theory, H2O cannot be oxidized to
OH in the photocatalytic system with NQML-8. But the presence of
OH have been detected by the capture experiment and ESR spectroscopy. Combined with the generation path of free radical, it can be speculated that there may be heterogeneous Fenton reactions in the system (Eq.
(11) and Eq.
(12) ), in which H2O2 may come from dissolved O2 in the solution (Eq.
(2) ).
(11)
Fe(III) + e- → Fe(II)
(12)
Fe(II) + H2O2 → Fe(III) + OH– +
OH

The concentrations of the in situ-generated H2O2 in the systems with NQML-8 and MIL-101(Fe) were detected by spectrophotometry with Copper(II) ion and DMP so as to verify the above conjecture [38]. As Fig. 8
a shows, the concentration of H2O2 produced in “ NQML-8 + vis” system is stronger than that of “MIL-101(Fe) + vis” system, reaching 0.31 and 0.24 mM, respectively. However, the concentration of H2O2 produced in NQML-8 system decreases to 0.13 mM when nitrogen is filled, indicating that the quantity of H2O2 in studied system is related to the content of dissolved oxygen. And the limited H2O2 generated in solution under N2 atmosphere may be due to the residual dissolved O2.

For purpose of further confirming the existence of photo Fenton effect in the studied system, the elemental states of Fe(III) and Fe(II) in NQML-8 before and after reaction were detected from the XPS Fe2p spectra. As illustrated in Fig. 8 b, before reaction, the signals located at 710.5 and 713.1 eV can be observed in NQML-8, both of which are related to the characteristic peaks of Fe(III) = O bond [37]. As is expected, the characteristic peak of Fe(II) appears at 709.9 eV after reaction [66]. It can be explained that the Fe(III) on NQML-8 surface is reduced into Fe(II) during the in-situ photo-Fenton process. Furthermore, the amount of Fe(II) generated on MIL-101(Fe) surface and NQML-8 were also investigated according to previous work [67], both of which gradually increase as the reaction proceeds (as depicted in Fig. 8 c). However, NQML-8 (0.49 mg·g−1) always possesses a higher Fe(II) content than MIL-101(Fe) (0.40 mg·g−1). This is because the presence of NCQDs promotes the rapid transfer of photogenerated electrons on MIL-101(Fe), which helps to produce Fe(II) upon visible light exposure [68].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Transformation intermediates and environmental risk for TC degradation
ce:section.ce:para:
The toxicity evolution of intermediates was predicted by T.E.S.T program for the consideration of their potential environmental risks, whose results were illustrated in Table. S1. The acute toxicity LD50 of the intermediates (Fig. 10
a) suggests that most of the intermediate products display lower toxicity than TC except for P15, P22 and P23, but the three high toxic intermediates are not the final products of TC degradation, indicating the low toxicity of transformation products can be achieved in the process of TC photocatalytic degradation by NQML-8. Additionally, despite the fact that the bioaccumulation factors of a few products are even higher than that of TC (Fig. 10 b), the developmental toxicity and mutagenicity of these intermediates especially the final product (P18, P25) have shown nontoxic and negative trends, respectively (Fig. 10 c and Fig. 10 d). Based on the above results, the NQML-8 photocatalytic system can be considered to be an effective approach to reduce the environmental risk of TC.

Combined with the above information, a brief mechanism of visible light catalytic degradation of TC by NQML is presented. As illustrated in Fig. 11
, the light with a longer wavelength can be transferred into shorter wavelength light under visible light on account of the up-converted capability of NCQDs. Afterwards, electrons in MIL-101(Fe) can be excited to its conduction band by the short wavelength light while holes are left on its valance band. The photogenerated electrons not only can activate O2 into O2
- and H2O2, but also can reduce Fe(III) to Fe(II) in MIL-101(Fe). Thus, a heterogeneous photo-Fenton can be formed with the in situ-generated H2O2 and Fe(II). Then, O2
- and h+ generated by the above photocatalytic reaction, and the
OH produced by the photo-Fenton reaction are all involved in TC degradation, making the highly toxic pollutant transformed into non-toxic products. This reaction system can construct a dual effect of photocatalytic and photo-Fenton processes towards TC removal.

In summary, a nitrogen doped carbon quantum dots modified composite catalyst NCQDs/MIL-101(Fe) (NQML) was successfully fabricated via the solvent impregnation method. As evidenced by the characterizations and experimental results, the introduction of NCQDs significantly enhanced the visible light up-conversion property, electron-hole separation and transfer capacity of MIL-101(Fe), which resulted in its effective use of the full spectrum of visible light and more efficient degradation rate of TC. Furthermore, the adding volume of NCQDs could effectively influence the photocatalytic activities of NQML-x composites and NQML-8 displayed the optimum photocatalytic performance among those studied catalysts. The TC degradation kinetic constant in the system with NQML-8 was as 15.85 and 8.24 folds of that in the systems with NCQDs and MIL-101(Fe), respectively. The ESR measurements and the trapping experiments confirmed that
OH, O2
-and h+ made major contributions for the degradation of TC. The possible mechanism proved that NQML could not only produce h+ but also activate O2 to in situ produce O2
- and H2O2 under visible light. The generated H2O2 was able to react with Fe(II) in MIL-101(Fe) to produce
OH, establishing a dual effect of photocatalytic and photo-Fenton processes. The identification of transformation intermediates revealed that the TC was finally transformed into the environmental friendly small-molecule organic compounds and then could be ulteriorly mineralized to CO2 and H2O. We anticipate that this study can offer new insights of constructing a dual effect of photocatalysis and photo-Fenton in sustainable wastewater treatment.
